title: Hannibal's Crossing
subtitle: Over the Alps and Into Italy, 218 BC
tagline: Rome controlled every approach to Italy. Hannibal marched forty thousand soldiers and thirty-seven war elephants over the Alps in November. Rome didn't see him coming because what he did was impossible.
villain_mandate: Hannibal must feel like a force of nature. The Alps crossing alone should be terrifying. Every obstacle — the altitude, the snow, the hostile mountain tribes, the logistics of feeding forty thousand men — must feel insurmountable. The arrival in Italy after all of this must feel triumphant against impossible odds.

scenes.title: What Rome Controlled
scenes.narration: 218 BC. Rome controlled every route into Italy. The sea: Roman navy. The coastal road through southern Gaul: Roman allies. The mountain passes: considered impassable in winter. Rome's strategy for the Second Punic War was simple — attack Carthage in Spain and Africa, fight the war away from Italy. They had never considered that someone would come over the mountains. Nobody had done it. Nobody would try it. Then Hannibal tried it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. A map of the western Mediterranean — Rome's routes guarded: sea routes blocked by the navy, coastal roads controlled by allies. The Alps shown as a wall at the top of Italy, marked IMPASSABLE. Then: a single line beginning in Spain, moving through southern Gaul, heading directly toward the Alps. Hannibal's route, impossible on paper. 16:9.

scenes.title: From Spain to the Rhône
scenes.narration: Hannibal left Cartagena, Spain in May 218 BC with ninety thousand infantry, twelve thousand cavalry, and thirty-seven war elephants. By the time he reached the Rhône River in southern Gaul, he had forty thousand infantry and nine thousand cavalry left — the rest lost to combat with Gallic tribes and desertion. He crossed the Rhône on rafts, fighting off Gallic attacks on both banks. He had lost a third of his army before he'd even seen the Alps.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hannibal's army marching through southern Gaul — vast but diminishing. The Rhône River crossing: elephants on giant rafts, some panicking and swimming, soldiers on improvised boats while Gallic warriors attack from the far bank. The chaos and scale of crossing a major river with an army and thirty-seven elephants. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Wall of Mountains
scenes.narration: October 218 BC. Hannibal's army reached the foot of the Alps. Gallic guides who had promised to lead them through the passes vanished. Mountain tribes attacked from the heights, rolling boulders onto the column. The path was barely wide enough for four men abreast. Horses and elephants fell off ledges. Men died of cold before they'd gained a thousand feet. The Alps were not a mountain range. They were a killing ground.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The army at the foot of the Alps — the mountains towering above them, the passes barely visible. Mountain tribe warriors on the heights above, rolling boulders down onto the column. Soldiers and pack animals on a path barely wide enough to walk. An elephant losing its footing on a narrow ledge. The scale of the obstacle: these mountains were not meant to be crossed by an army. 16:9.

scenes.title: Nine Days to the Top
scenes.narration: Nine days to reach the summit. At the top, there was fresh snow on old ice. The path had become a glassy sheet. Men fell. Animals fell. Equipment was lost. Hannibal called the army together at the summit and pointed down — Italy was visible below. He told them: everything you can see is yours for the taking. Then they had to get down. The descent, on icy slopes, was harder than the ascent.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. THE SUMMIT — Hannibal's army at the top of the Alps, exhausted, reduced, in the cold. Hannibal pointing down at Italy spread below — the Po Valley green and vast, the prize visible. The soldiers looking at it. Then: the descent on ice-slicked slopes, soldiers sliding, equipment lost, the same kind of death from below as from above. 16:9.

scenes.title: Moving a Mountain
scenes.narration: On the descent, a massive rockfall blocked the path — an entire section of cliff had collapsed, creating a wall of rock and debris that no army could cross. Hannibal ordered fires lit against the rock face. They burned for hours. Then they poured vinegar on the superheated stone. The thermal shock cracked the rock. His engineers moved the pieces. It took three days. He moved a mountain because he had to.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The rockfall blocking the descent — a wall of shattered stone, impassable. Soldiers building fires against the rock face. After hours: the rock glowing with heat. Vinegar being poured — the crack and fracture of superheated stone in cold liquid. Engineers moving the broken pieces. An army making a road where there was none. 16:9.

scenes.title: Into Italy
scenes.narration: Fifteen days. That's how long the crossing took. Hannibal entered Italy with twenty-six thousand men — less than a third of the force that had left Spain. Most of his horses were dead or unfit for battle. Many of his war elephants had died of cold. He had crossed an impossible mountain range in late autumn, in snow and ice, with an army including thirty-seven elephants, through hostile tribes, with no supply line and no fallback position. He had made it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hannibal's army emerging from the last Alpine pass into the Po Valley — the survivors, visibly reduced, worn, some frostbitten. But in formation. Still an army. Italy spread before them: green, flat, warm after the mountains. Hannibal on horseback at the front, one eye lost to the marsh fever he'll develop later, looking at the country he has come to destroy. 16:9.

scenes.title: Rome Cannot Believe It
scenes.narration: The Roman consul Publius Cornelius Scipio had sailed to Spain to intercept Hannibal before he crossed. He arrived in Spain to find Hannibal already gone. He sailed back, marched north, and met Hannibal at the Trebia River — where Hannibal destroyed his army. Rome sent its message to the Senate: Hannibal is in Italy. He came over the mountains. They didn't believe it until they saw the elephants.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Roman senate receiving the report — senators in disbelief. Then: Roman scouts approaching Hannibal's camp and seeing the war elephants in the Italian countryside — the most alien thing imaginable in northern Italy. Their faces: this is real. He's here. He's in Italy with elephants. The disbelief becoming fear. 16:9.

scenes.title: Why He Did It
scenes.narration: Hannibal's crossing wasn't just a military maneuver. It was a statement. Rome's defensive strategy depended on the Alps being impassable. By crossing them, Hannibal proved that Rome's assumptions about safety were wrong. He wanted to break Rome's Italian allies — to show them that Rome couldn't protect them. He had fifteen years in Italy. No reinforcements. No supply line from Carthage. He would have to live off Roman land and win Roman allies away from Rome.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hannibal's army moving through northern Italy — Italian allied towns watching from their walls. Some gates opening in welcome. Others closing. Hannibal's strategic situation: deep in enemy territory, no supply line, fifteen years ahead of him. His certainty despite the odds: everything depends on making Italy's allies choose him over Rome. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Greatest March in Military History
scenes.narration: Military historians still debate which route Hannibal used. The Alpine passes are still studied in military academies as the definitive example of logistical genius under impossible conditions. Napoleon crossed the Alps in 1800 — in spring, with modern equipment — and called Hannibal's crossing the greatest military achievement in history. The elephants that survived it were used at Trebia, Trasimene, and Cannae. They were legend.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The alpine landscape — empty, beautiful, the pass Hannibal used still there. A timeline showing subsequent great commanders who studied this crossing: Napoleon at the Saint Bernard Pass looking at the Alps and thinking of Hannibal. Military academy maps with Hannibal's route marked and studied. The pass itself: the most famous road never built. 16:9.

scenes.title: Over the Impossible
scenes.narration: Thirty-seven war elephants. Forty thousand soldiers. November. The Alps. No supply line. No retreat. Hostile tribes on the slopes above him. He lost half his force getting through. He arrived with twenty-six thousand men and immediately won three of the greatest battles in Roman history. Some generals fight within what's possible. Hannibal decided what was possible. Subscribe to Gods & Glory for history's most extraordinary campaigns.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — an alpine pass at sunset, the view from the summit: Italy on one side, the rest of the world on the other. One path, barely visible, worn by centuries of weather. Hannibal walked this with thirty-seven elephants. 16:9.

viral_hook: Rome controlled every approach to Italy. So Hannibal took forty thousand soldiers and thirty-seven war elephants over the Alps. In November. In snow.
highlight_note: Scene 5 — fire and vinegar on the rock wall. He moved a mountain because he had to. Visual payoff of the whole episode.
youtube_title: Hannibal Crossed the Alps in Winter With 37 War Elephants | Gods & Glory
lesson: The most dangerous move is the one your enemy considers impossible. Hannibal crossed the Alps because Rome had decided no one would try.
